title: Product BOM Decomposition: GfS Türwächter IoT

sections.title: Market Context: Hotels (except Casino Hotels) and Motels — NAICS 721110
sections.content:
*BOM covers the physical product manufactured by GfS Rettungswegtechnik (Assa Abloy Group), NAICS 332321 (Metal Window and Door Manufacturing). Hotels market shapes variant selection and alternative prioritization. Source: 01_product_decomposition.md, 02_functional_promise.md, 03_constraints.md, 05_market_discovery.md.*

---

sections.title: Routing Confirmation
sections.content:
| Check | Result |
|---|---|
| Manufacturer NAICS | 332321 — Metal Window and Door Manufacturing (sector 33) ✓ BOM applies |
| Output type | Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring |
| Relevant variant | Exit Control 179 Pre-alarm + Radio (Art. 990140) — lever handle, wireless, IoT |
| Process VN boundary | Process VN covers HOW the device is made (stamping, molding, assembly). BOM starts below L5 — at the subsystems inside the device the hotel buys. |
| Hotel-specific constraint focus | C5 (surface-mount aesthetics), C6 (EasyWave protocol lock-in vs. hotel BMS), C4 (battery life) |

---

sections.title: B.1 — L4 Subsystems
sections.content:
```
OUTPUT TYPE (L5 — Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT)
|
+-- L4  Mechanical locking/release subsystem
|       Spring-loaded guard arm physically blocks door handle in locked state.
|       Swing-release (45°) activates alarm via mechanical linkage.
|       PRIMARY FUNCTIONAL CORE.
|
+-- L4  Acoustic alarm subsystem
|       Piezoelectric horn + resonance cavity produces 95 dB alarm on guard activation.
|       Mechanical trigger linkage: operates at 0 V (no battery required).
|       AUXILIARY FUNCTIONAL (regulatory-critical: must be power-independent).
|
+-- L4  IoT telemetry subsystem
|       RF transceiver detects guard state, transmits events to wireless receiver.
|       Battery supplies power to electronics only. Alarm is battery-independent.
|       CONTROL / INTERFACE.
|
+-- L4  Enclosure/mounting subsystem
        RAL 6029 housing contains and protects all components.
        Surface-mount back plate attaches to existing door handle without drilling.
        CONTAINMENT / STRUCTURAL.
```

**Augmentation check:** Does the product have a packaging subsystem? No — the device IS its own housing (the L4 enclosure is the final form). Does it have an energy system? Battery is part of the IoT subsystem, not a standalone subsystem, because the primary alarm function requires zero power.

---

sections.title: B.2 + B.3 — Full Decomposition with Alternatives
sections.content:
### L4: Mechanical locking/release subsystem

**L4 Alternatives — Guard mechanism type**

| Alternative | Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Spring-loaded swing-release guard (mechanical-primary) | ~65% | Stable | MEDIUM | Analyst estimate — dominant in EU emergency exit alarm guard category |
| Electromagnetic detent + spring-release (powered hold) | ~20% | Growing | LOW | Analyst estimate; dormakaba SafeRoute, GEZE-class systems |
| Magnetic latch with alarm trigger (no physical blocker) | ~10% | Stable | LOW | Analyst estimate |
| Hydraulic/pneumatic resistance guard | ~5% | Declining | LOW | Analyst estimate; legacy industrial use |

> **Hotel implication:** Spring-loaded mechanical-primary is preferred for hotels due to power-independence mandate (C2) and retrofit simplicity (C5).

---

#### L3: Guard arm mechanism

**L3 Alternatives — Guard arm construction material**

| Alternative | Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Zinc die-cast guard arm | ~55% | Stable | MEDIUM | Door hardware material data; zinc alloy dominant in volume segment |
| Engineering polymer (PA66 / glass-filled nylon) | ~30% | Growing | MEDIUM | Lighter weight; cost reduction trend in security hardware |
| Aluminum die-cast guard arm | ~15% | Stable | LOW | Analyst estimate; higher cost, premium segment |

##### L2: Pivot/spring assembly

**L2 Alternatives — Pivot spring configuration**

| Alternative | Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Torsion spring (coiled, around pivot axis) | ~70% | Stable | LOW | Analyst estimate; standard for angular return mechanisms |
| Leaf spring (flat, cantilever) | ~25% | Stable | LOW | Simpler tooling; used in lower-cost variants |
| Compression spring + lever | ~5% | Declining | LOW | Analyst estimate |

**L1 → L0: Torsion spring**

| L0 Raw Material | Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Spring steel (carbon steel, high-tensile) | ~90% | Stable | MEDIUM | Standard for small coil/torsion springs; cost-effective, reliable fatigue life |
| Stainless steel (304/316) | ~10% | Stable | MEDIUM | IP45 outdoor / humid environments; corrosion-resistant premium |

##### L2: Handle engagement bracket

**L2 Alternatives — Handle interface method**

| Alternative | Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Universal U-bracket (fits most lever handles) | ~65% | Stable | LOW | Analyst estimate; retrofit-friendly |
| Lever-specific saddle bracket | ~25% | Stable | LOW | Better fit for standardized EN 179 lever geometries |
| Custom OEM-profiled bracket | ~10% | Declining | LOW | Legacy purpose-built installations |

**L1 → L0: Bracket material**

| L0 Raw Material | Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Zinc die-cast (ZA-8 or Zamak alloys) | ~55% | Stable | MEDIUM | Cost-effective; standard in door hardware fittings; ~69% of volume-based hardware uses zinc alloy (Grand View Research, Door Hardware Market 2024) |
| Stainless steel (stamped, 304) | ~30% | Growing | MEDIUM | Corrosion resistance; premium segment; ~38% of global door hardware by material volume (Verified Market Research 2024) |
| Carbon steel (zinc-plated) | ~15% | Declining | MEDIUM | Lowest cost; declining due to corrosion concerns |

#### L3: Key override mechanism

**L3 Alternatives — Override cylinder type**

| Alternative | Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Profile half-cylinder (DIN EN 1303) | ~75% | Stable | MEDIUM | European standard; locksmith-compatible; standard on Türwächter |
| Oval cylinder (Scandinavian / ANSI format) | ~15% | Stable | LOW | Analyst estimate; Nordic and US markets |
| Cam lock / furniture cylinder | ~8% | Declining | LOW | Analyst estimate; low-security legacy applications |
| No key override (alarm-only) | ~2% | Stable | LOW | Analyst estimate; lowest-cost mechanical variants |

**L1 → L0: Cylinder housing material**

| L0 Raw Material | Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Brass (CuZn39Pb3) | ~65% | Stable | MEDIUM | Traditional key cylinder material; machinability, corrosion resistance |
| Zinc alloy (die-cast) | ~25% | Stable | MEDIUM | Cost reduction; slightly lower durability |
| Stainless steel | ~10% | Growing | LOW | Analyst estimate; high-security / corrosive environments |

---

### L4: Acoustic alarm subsystem

**L4 Alternatives — Alarm transducer type**

| Alternative | Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Piezoelectric alarm horn (electromechanical disc) | ~70% | Stable | MEDIUM | Dominant in door alarm hardware; low power, high SPL, wide temperature range; see Dataintelo Alarm Buzzer Market 2025 |
| Electromagnetic buzzer/sounder | ~25% | Declining | MEDIUM | Electromagnetic buzzer global market $1.35B (2024); declining in safety-critical applications due to power dependence (Cognitive Market Research 2024) |
| MEMS acoustic transducer | ~3% | Emerging | LOW | Commercially available but not yet scaled in safety hardware |
| Mechanical striker/bell (fully passive) | ~2% | Declining | LOW | Analyst estimate; no longer meets 95 dB requirements cost-effectively |

> **Critical constraint:** The alarm transducer must operate at 0 V (C2 — absolute constraint). This eliminates all alternatives that require continuous power. Only piezoelectric disc and mechanical striker pass this constraint. The 70% piezo dominance reflects regulatory selection pressure.

#### L3: Alarm transducer module

##### L2: Piezoelectric disc assembly

**L1 → L0: Piezo disc element material**

| L0 Raw Material | Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| PZT ceramic (Lead Zirconate Titanate) | ~85% | Stable | MEDIUM | Industry standard piezo material; high piezoelectric coefficient; used in virtually all commercial alarm horns |
| Lead-free BaTiO3 (Barium Titanate) ceramic | ~10% | Growing | MEDIUM | RoHS/REACH compliance push; lower coefficient but improving |
| PVDF polymer film | ~5% | Niche | LOW | Analyst estimate; flexible, low voltage — used in thin transducers |

**L1 → L0: Acoustic resonance chamber material**

| L0 Raw Material | Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ABS polymer | ~70% | Stable | MEDIUM | Cost, moldability, acoustic properties; dominant in electronic housings |
| PC/ABS blend | ~20% | Stable | MEDIUM | Better impact resistance; used in higher-grade devices |
| Glass-filled nylon (PA66-GF) | ~10% | Growing | LOW | Analyst estimate; dimensional stability at temperature extremes |

##### L2: Mechanical trigger linkage

**L1 → L0: Trigger arm material**

| L0 Raw Material | Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Stainless steel (stamped, 304) | ~70% | Stable | MEDIUM | Corrosion resistance; fatigue-critical component in safety device |
| Zinc-plated carbon steel | ~25% | Declining | MEDIUM | Cost reduction; corrosion risk in humid environments (hotel stairwells) |
| Engineering polymer (POM / acetal) | ~5% | Growing | LOW | Analyst estimate; weight reduction; creep risk at elevated load |

---

### L4: IoT telemetry subsystem

**L4 Alternatives — Wireless radio protocol**

| Alternative | Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Proprietary sub-GHz (e.g., 868 MHz EasyWave) | ~35% | Declining | MEDIUM | Dominant in legacy installed base of European door alarm hardware; no open standard interoperability |
| BLE (Bluetooth Low Energy, 2.4 GHz) | ~30% | Growing | MEDIUM | 4.8B devices powered by BLE in 2024; 60% of smart building/lighting automation providers adopting BLE mesh (IHS Markit Connectivity Report) |
| Zigbee (IEEE 802.15.4, 2.4 GHz mesh) | ~15% | Stable | MEDIUM | 1.4B active Zigbee devices globally (2024); strong in building automation and access control (SkyQuest Zigbee Market 2025) |
| LoRaWAN (Sub-GHz LPWAN) | ~12% | Growing | MEDIUM | $9.08B LoRa/LoRaWAN market (2025), growing rapidly for large-campus IoT (360iResearch 2026) |
| Z-Wave (Sub-GHz mesh) | ~5% | Stable | LOW | Analyst estimate; niche in residential/small commercial; less common in hospitality |
| Wi-Fi (802.11) | ~3% | Stable | LOW | Analyst estimate; high power draw; rarely used for door-mounted IoT |

> **Hotel implication:** Hotels increasingly standardize on BACnet/IP or KNX BMS platforms. EasyWave's proprietary protocol creates C6 lock-in. BLE and Zigbee alternatives are gaining share in new hotel builds; LoRaWAN suits large resort/campus properties. GfS wireless BUS gateway partially bridges C6 but does not eliminate it.

#### L3: Radio communication module

##### L2: RF transceiver circuit assembly

**L2 Alternatives — Antenna type**

| Alternative | Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| PCB trace antenna (inverted-F or meander) | ~55% | Stable | MEDIUM | Lowest cost; adequate for 30 m range requirement; no additional components |
| Ceramic chip antenna | ~30% | Growing | MEDIUM | Better radiation pattern in constrained enclosures; preferred for sub-GHz |
| Whip/wire stub antenna | ~15% | Declining | MEDIUM | Better range but requires protrusion from housing; cosmetically undesirable in hotel context |

**L1 → L0: PCB substrate**

| L0 Raw Material | Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| FR4 (epoxy resin + woven glass fiber) | ~85% | Stable | HIGH | Industry standard for IoT device PCBs; cost-effective, well-characterized |
| Rogers/PTFE high-frequency laminate | ~10% | Stable | LOW | Analyst estimate; used only where RF performance is critical at high frequency |
| Flexible polyimide (FPC) | ~5% | Growing | LOW | Analyst estimate; space-constrained folded assemblies |

#### L3: State detection module

**L3 Alternatives — Guard-position sensor technology**

| Alternative | Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Reed switch + permanent magnet | ~60% | Stable | MEDIUM | Lowest cost, fully passive, proven in alarm hardware; sealed glass tube |
| Hall-effect sensor (solid-state) | ~30% | Growing | MEDIUM | No moving parts; better vibration resistance; requires 0.1–5 mA standby |
| Optical interrupt sensor | ~8% | Stable | LOW | Analyst estimate; used in precision applications; dust sensitivity |
| Mechanical microswitch | ~2% | Declining | LOW | Analyst estimate; contact bounce, wear-limited |

**L1 → L0: Permanent magnet (reed switch variant)**

| L0 Raw Material | Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| NdFeB (Neodymium Iron Boron, sintered) | ~75% | Stable | MEDIUM | Highest energy density; standard for small magnet applications in electronics |
| Ferrite (BaFe12O19) | ~25% | Declining | MEDIUM | Lower energy density; cost advantage in price-sensitive applications |

#### L3: Power supply module

**L3 Alternatives — Primary battery chemistry**

| Alternative | Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Alkaline 9V block (MnO2/Zn) | ~60% | Declining | MEDIUM | Market standard; alkaline held 63.5% of primary battery market in 2025 (Mordor Intelligence); but declining in IoT applications |
| Lithium primary 9V (LiSO2 or LiMnO2) | ~30% | Growing | MEDIUM | 3× energy density vs. alkaline; better cold-temperature performance; preferred for critical safety devices; lithium primary CAGR 9.5% through 2031 (Mordor Intelligence Primary Battery Market) |
| Lithium coin cell array (CR2032 × N) | ~8% | Stable | LOW | Analyst estimate; used in ultra-compact IoT sensors; capacity limited for alarm horn |
| Rechargeable NiMH 9V | ~2% | Niche | LOW | Analyst estimate; self-discharge problematic for infrequently monitored doors |

> **Hotel implication:** Lithium primary strongly preferred for hotels — better battery life at moderate traffic levels, wider temperature range (hotel stairwells can be cold), and higher predictive maintenance reliability (C4 mitigation).

**L1 → L0: Alkaline 9V cell composition**

| L0 Raw Material | Role | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| MnO2 (Manganese Dioxide) | Cathode active material | ~40% of cell mass |
| Zinc powder (Zn) | Anode active material | ~25% |
| KOH solution | Electrolyte | ~15% |
| Carbon/graphite | Current collector | ~10% |
| Steel/nickel | Cell casing | ~10% |

*(No meaningful design-choice alternatives at L0 within alkaline chemistry — stop at L1 for alkaline.)*

**L1 → L0: Lithium primary 9V cell**

| L0 Raw Material | Role | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| LiMnO2 or LiSO2 active material | Cathode + anode | ~55% of cell energy |
| Organic electrolyte (LiCF3SO3 / LiClO4) | Ion transport | ~20% |
| Aluminum/steel casing | Housing | ~15% |
| Carbon/graphite | Current collector | ~10% |

---

### L4: Enclosure/mounting subsystem

**L4 Alternatives — Housing form factor**

| Alternative | Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Surface-mounted polymer housing (snap-fit assembly) | ~70% | Stable | MEDIUM | Dominant in door alarm hardware; retrofit-ready, no drilling |
| Surface-mounted die-cast metal housing | ~20% | Declining | MEDIUM | Heavier, higher cost; used in industrial/vandal-resistant applications |
| Semi-recessed polymer housing (requires shallow door channel) | ~7% | Growing | LOW | Analyst estimate; emerging in premium hospitality to reduce protrusion (C5 mitigation) |
| Flush-mount integrated housing (door modification required) | ~3% | Niche | LOW | Analyst estimate; architecturally invisible but violates retrofit constraint |

> **Hotel implication:** Surface-mounted polymer housing is the standard. For 4–5-star hotels, C5 (visible protrusion) creates aesthetic friction. Semi-recessed alternatives are gaining share as premium hospitality increasingly specifies minimalist door hardware aesthetics.

#### L3: Primary housing module

**L3 Alternatives — Shell material**

| Alternative | Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ABS (Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene) | ~40% | Stable | MEDIUM | Cost-effective, good impact resistance, easy to color (RAL 6029); dominant in electronic enclosures |
| PC/ABS blend (Polycarbonate/ABS) | ~35% | Growing | MEDIUM | Better UV stability and impact resistance vs. pure ABS; preferred for exposed applications |
| PA66-GF (Glass-filled Nylon, 30%) | ~15% | Growing | LOW | Analyst estimate; dimensional stability, higher stiffness; growing in precision hardware |
| PP/ABS blend | ~10% | Declining | LOW | Analyst estimate; lower cost, reduced performance |

**L1 → L0: Surface coating (RAL 6029 Safety Green)**

| L0 Raw Material | Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Polyurethane powder coat | ~70% | Stable | MEDIUM | Industry standard for safety-colored hardware; durable, scratch-resistant |
| Liquid polyurethane paint (spray) | ~20% | Declining | MEDIUM | Lower tooling cost; less durability |
| Integral pigmented polymer (co-injection/colored resin) | ~10% | Growing | LOW | Analyst estimate; no secondary coating step; color stability over UV exposure |

#### L3: Mounting hardware module

**L2: Door attachment assembly — Screw material**

| L0 Raw Material | Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Stainless steel (A2-70, 304) | ~60% | Growing | MEDIUM | Corrosion resistance; preferred for surface-mounted hardware in humid stairwells |
| Zinc-plated carbon steel | ~35% | Declining | MEDIUM | Cost-effective; corrosion risk in hotel stairwell environments |
| Polymer/nylon (for glass-frame variant) | ~5% | Stable | LOW | Analyst estimate; non-metallic for glass-panel doors |

**L2: Glass-frame mounting plate (optional — Art. 991170)**

| L0 Raw Material | Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Steel (stamped, powder-coated) | ~80% | Stable | LOW | Analyst estimate; standard adapter plate material |
| Aluminum (extruded) | ~20% | Growing | LOW | Analyst estimate; lighter, architectural aesthetic |

---

sections.title: B.4 — Component Category Map: Radio Protocol Position
sections.content:
Most relevant for hotel market (C6 constraint).

```
COMPONENT CATEGORY: Wireless Radio Transmitter

Position 1: L4 IoT telemetry subsystem > L3 Radio communication module > L2 RF transceiver circuit assembly
            Function: Broadcasts guard state (locked/unlocked) and alarm events to wireless receiver ~30m
            Safety-critical: NO (supervisory overlay; mechanical alarm functions independently)
            Hotel-specific: Must eventually integrate with hotel BMS (BACnet/IP or KNX)
            Alternatives at this position: EasyWave 868 MHz (35%), BLE (30%), Zigbee (15%), LoRaWAN (12%), Z-Wave (5%), Wi-Fi (3%)

INSIGHT: The same "wireless door status transmitter" role is filled by radically
different competing protocol ecosystems. EasyWave competes against BLE on power
consumption and range; against Zigbee on mesh coverage for multi-floor hotels;
against LoRaWAN for large resort campus applications. The Core Job is identical
(broadcast door state without mains power), but protocol selection locks the
operator into incompatible receiver/gateway ecosystems. Hotels evaluating this
product face a gateway vendor decision, not just a device decision.
```

---

sections.title: Quality Checks
sections.content:
| Check | Status |
|---|---|
| Every L4 subsystem decomposes to at least L2 | PASS — all 4 subsystems reach L1/L0 |
| Every alternative set sums to 100% (±1%) | PASS |
| Every alternative set has confidence + source | PASS |
| Mechanical alarm operates independently of power at every level | PASS — alarm subsystem documented as 0V operational; battery only in IoT subsystem |
| BOM vs. VN boundary maintained | PASS — no manufacturing process steps in BOM; all components end up IN the product |
| Packaging subsystem | N/A — device is its own housing; L4 enclosure covers containment |
| Raw materials (L0) identified where material choice matters | PASS — battery chemistry, piezo material, guard arm L0 documented |
| CFJ traceability | PASS — each subsystem traces upward: IoT subsystem → Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard → CFJ-1 (ensure emergency exit compliance while preventing unauthorized door use) |

---

entities.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

bom_units.name: Mechanical locking/release subsystem
bom_units.description: Spring-loaded guard arm physically blocks door handle; swing-release activates alarm via mechanical linkage at 0V
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

children.name: Guard arm mechanism
children.description: Structural arm that blocks handle and swings to release

children.name: Pivot/spring assembly
children.description: Torsion spring + pivot axle enabling swing-release motion

children.name: Torsion spring
children.description: Coiled spring providing return force after guard release

children.name: Pivot axle
children.description: Rotation axis for guard arm swing

children.name: Guard arm body
children.description: Structural casting forming the physical handle blocker

children.name: Handle engagement bracket
children.description: Bracket interface attaching guard to existing door lever handle

children.name: Key override mechanism
children.description: Profile half-cylinder lock enabling authorized bypass without alarm trigger

children.name: Lock cylinder receiver
children.description: Housing accepting DIN EN 1303 profile cylinder

children.name: Lock cam
children.description: Cam element translating key rotation to guard release

bom_units.name: Acoustic alarm subsystem
bom_units.description: Piezoelectric horn + mechanical trigger producing 95 dB alarm; operates at 0V — no battery dependency
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

children.name: Alarm transducer module
children.description: Piezoelectric disc + resonance cavity producing audible output

children.name: Piezo disc element
children.description: PZT ceramic disc — primary acoustic transducer

children.name: Mechanical trigger linkage
children.description: Actuator arm + return spring coupling guard swing to alarm trigger

children.name: Trigger arm
children.description: Stainless steel arm transmitting guard motion to piezo trigger

children.name: Return spring
children.description: Spring resetting trigger on guard close

bom_units.name: IoT telemetry subsystem
bom_units.description: RF transceiver broadcasts guard state and alarm events; battery powers electronics only
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

children.name: Radio communication module
children.description: 868 MHz EasyWave transceiver transmitting to wireless receiver ≤30m

children.name: State detection module
children.description: Sensor detecting guard position (locked/unlocked) and triggering transmission

children.name: Power supply module
children.description: 9V block battery powering electronics; mechanical alarm is battery-independent

bom_units.name: Enclosure/mounting subsystem

positions.bom_path: L4 IoT telemetry subsystem > L3 Radio communication module > L2 RF transceiver circuit assembly
positions.function: Broadcasts guard state and alarm events to wireless receiver
positions.hotel_note: Protocol selection determines BMS integration path; EasyWave requires GfS-specific gateway for BACnet/KNX hotel systems (C6)
